title: Key-Value Store - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a Dynamo-style key-value store that scales horizontally, survives failures, and exposes tunable consistency.
problem: Build a distributed key-value store that supports put(key, value) and get(key) for small opaque values, with high availability, low latency, automatic scaling, and configurable consistency.

functional: Store and retrieve opaque values by key.
Support values up to roughly 10 KB.
Replicate data across multiple nodes and data centers.
Expose tunable consistency for reads and writes.
Recover from temporary node failures without rejecting most traffic.
nonFunctional: Low p99 latency for reads and writes.
High availability under node and rack failures.
Eventual consistency with conflict detection when the system chooses availability.
Operational visibility for membership, repair, and data movement.
requirements.designPrompt: Use the interview to move from a single-node hash table to a replicated, partitioned, repairable distributed store. Call out where the design chooses availability, where callers can ask for stronger reads, and how failures are repaired later.

capacity.label: Replication factor
capacity.value: N = 3 as a default starting point

capacity.label: Quorum examples
capacity.value: R=2, W=2 for stronger reads; R=1, W=1 for lower latency and higher availability

capacity.label: Repair cadence
capacity.value: Continuous hinted handoff plus background anti-entropy

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: Values are small enough to keep hot metadata and indexes in memory while storing durable bytes on disk. Traffic is write-heavy enough that append-only writes and compaction matter. The system runs across multiple availability zones and eventually multiple regions. Keys are evenly hashed before placement.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Capacity is dominated by replicated storage, compaction write amplification, and tail latency during repair. Keep quorum math visible because it drives both latency and consistency.

dataModel.name: kv_item
dataModel.note: Logical value version stored for a key.

dataModel.name: vector_clock_entry
dataModel.note: Tracks causal versions by writer or replica.

dataModel.name: node_membership
dataModel.note: Current physical node status and token ownership.

dataModel.name: hinted_handoff
dataModel.note: Temporary write stored for a replica that was unavailable.

steps.title: 1. Scope the Contract Around Key, Value, and Consistency
steps.description: Start by narrowing the product surface to get, put, and delete. The important design choice is that values are opaque to the store, while the API exposes enough version context for conflict handling.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the API hide consistency completely, or should clients choose read and write consistency per request?

concepts.definition: The storage service does not interpret the value bytes.
concepts.whyItMatters: This keeps the core store generic and avoids coupling it to application schemas.

concepts.definition: The caller can trade latency and availability against stronger read-after-write behavior.
concepts.whyItMatters: This is the main product lever for a Dynamo-style store.

concepts.definition: Metadata returned with a read and sent with a later write.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the store detect concurrent writes instead of overwriting them silently.

flows.name: Simple get and put
flows.note: The first flow intentionally ignores distribution so the API contract stays clear.

deepDives: A single-node hash table is a useful first diagram because it exposes the API semantics, but it fails availability and scale immediately.
Do not promise strict consistency by default unless the rest of the design pays for synchronous quorum reads and writes.
bottlenecks: Single machine memory and disk limits.
Single failure domain.
No graceful way to rebalance keys.
talkingPoints: Mention that key validation and authentication can sit at the API boundary but are not the core distributed-systems problem.

steps.title: 2. Replace the Hash Table With a Log-Structured Storage Engine
steps.description: A durable key-value node needs fast writes and efficient reads. A commit log, memtable, SSTables, and Bloom filters provide the local storage engine used by many distributed stores.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should each node update data in place, or use an append-only write path with compaction?

concepts.definition: An append-only durable log written before acknowledging a write.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects recent writes from process or machine crashes.

concepts.definition: An in-memory sorted structure that buffers recent writes.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives fast writes and fast reads for hot recent data.

concepts.definition: An immutable sorted table flushed to disk.
concepts.whyItMatters: Immutable files make compaction and repair easier.

concepts.definition: A probabilistic set-membership structure.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids unnecessary disk reads for keys absent from an SSTable.

flows.name: Local write path
flows.note: Writes are acknowledged after the commit log and memtable update, while disk tables are created asynchronously.

deepDives: This step separates local durability from distributed durability. Replication comes later; a replica still needs its own durable local write path.
bottlenecks: Compaction can create write amplification.
Too many SSTables increase read latency if Bloom filters and compaction are weak.
talkingPoints: Explain why the local engine is log-structured before introducing partitioning.

steps.title: 3. Partition Keys With Consistent Hashing and Virtual Nodes
steps.description: A coordinator hashes each key onto a token ring. Virtual nodes spread ownership across physical nodes and make node additions less disruptive.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should the system decide which node owns a key when machines are added and removed?

concepts.definition: A placement scheme where keys and nodes live on the same hash ring.
concepts.whyItMatters: It limits key movement when the cluster changes size.

concepts.definition: A token owned by a physical node, with many tokens per physical node.
concepts.whyItMatters: It smooths load and supports heterogeneous hardware.

concepts.definition: The node that receives a request and routes it to the correct replicas.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets clients avoid knowing the full partitioning algorithm.

flows.name: Partition lookup
flows.note: The coordinator maps the key to a token range before choosing replicas.

deepDives: Virtual nodes are the difference between a toy ring and a production ring because real machines and key distributions are not uniform.
bottlenecks: Hot keys still need caching or request coalescing.
Ring metadata must be consistent enough that coordinators do not route wildly differently.
talkingPoints: Use this step to explain why modulo hashing causes massive remapping.

steps.title: 4. Replicate Each Key and Use Quorums
steps.description: The coordinator writes to N replicas by walking clockwise on the ring. R and W determine how many replicas must respond before a read or write succeeds.
steps.decisionPrompt: Do we optimize default operations for low latency availability, or require quorum overlap for stronger consistency?

concepts.definition: The number of replicas that should store each key.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects data when nodes fail.

concepts.definition: The number of replica responses required for a read.
concepts.whyItMatters: Higher R increases confidence that the latest version is seen.

concepts.definition: The number of replica acknowledgements required for a write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Higher W reduces the chance that a successful write is lost or stale.

concepts.definition: When R + W > N, at least one replica participates in both the read and write quorum.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the usual interview shortcut for explaining stronger consistency.

options.title: Low-latency default
options.description: Use R=1 and W=1 for fastest common-case operations, then repair inconsistencies asynchronously.
tradeoffs: Clients may read stale data after a successful write.

options.title: Quorum default
options.description: Use R=2 and W=2 with N=3 when the caller needs stronger read-after-write behavior.
tradeoffs: Increases latency and rejects more requests during failures.

flows.name: Quorum write
flows.note: The coordinator can return after the requested W acknowledgements, while remaining replicas catch up later.

deepDives: Strong consistency is not free. It is a per-operation tradeoff against latency and availability under partition.
bottlenecks: Tail latency is driven by the slowest replica required by the quorum.
Replica placement must avoid putting all copies in one failure domain.
talkingPoints: Name the CAP tradeoff explicitly but keep the answer grounded in R, W, and N.

steps.title: 5. Detect Concurrent Writes With Vector Clocks
steps.description: When the system accepts writes during partitions, concurrent versions can exist. Vector clocks let the store tell whether one version supersedes another or whether both must be returned.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the store resolve write conflicts automatically, or return siblings to the application?

concepts.definition: A compact causal history containing counters from writers or replicas.
concepts.whyItMatters: It identifies concurrent updates without requiring a global clock.

concepts.definition: Multiple live versions for the same key that cannot be causally ordered.
concepts.whyItMatters: They force explicit conflict resolution instead of silent data loss.

concepts.definition: A coordinator writes the newest resolved version back to stale replicas during a read.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gradually heals divergence on hot keys.

flows.name: Conflict read
flows.note: Concurrent versions are returned with their contexts so the application can merge or choose.

messages.label: siblings x and y with contexts

deepDives: Last-write-wins is tempting but dangerous when clocks skew. Vector clocks make the conflict visible.
bottlenecks: Vector clocks can grow and need pruning.
Application-level merge logic can be hard for complex values.
talkingPoints: Use a shopping cart example if asked why automatic overwrite is risky.

steps.title: 6. Handle Temporary Failures With Gossip and Hinted Handoff
steps.description: Nodes exchange membership state through gossip. When a replica is down, a nearby healthy node can temporarily store a hint and replay it after recovery.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should writes continue when one of the intended replicas is unavailable?

concepts.definition: Nodes periodically exchange what they know about membership and health.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids one central failure detector.

concepts.definition: A write succeeds on the first reachable healthy nodes rather than only the ideal replicas.
concepts.whyItMatters: It preserves availability during temporary failures.

concepts.definition: A healthy node stores a write intended for a failed replica and later hands it back.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes temporary failure recovery faster than waiting for full repair.

flows.name: Hinted handoff
flows.note: The write remains available, while ownership is corrected when the failed node returns.

deepDives: Hints are not a substitute for durable replication forever. They need expiration and monitoring.
bottlenecks: Hint holders can accumulate large backlogs.
Membership false positives can route too much traffic away from healthy nodes.
talkingPoints: Separate temporary failure handling from permanent divergence repair.

steps.title: 7. Repair Permanent Divergence With Merkle Trees
steps.description: Background anti-entropy compares compact hashes of key ranges between replicas. When a range differs, nodes exchange only the affected keys.
steps.decisionPrompt: How can replicas find and repair missing data without scanning and transferring everything?

concepts.definition: Background comparison and repair between replicas.
concepts.whyItMatters: It restores convergence after missed writes, disk loss, or long outages.

concepts.definition: A tree of hashes over key ranges.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets replicas find differing ranges efficiently.

concepts.definition: Merging immutable tables and removing overwritten or deleted values.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps read amplification and disk growth under control.

flows.name: Anti-entropy repair
flows.note: The repair job compares hashes top down, then streams only missing or stale keys.

deepDives: Merkle trees are useful because distributed stores repair continuously, not only after visible incidents.
bottlenecks: Repair consumes disk, network, and CPU.
Tombstones must be retained long enough so deletes are not resurrected.
talkingPoints: Mention that hot reads can trigger read repair, but cold ranges still need scheduled repair.

steps.title: 8. Add Multi-Datacenter Operations and Rebalancing
steps.description: The final production design places replicas across zones and regions, exposes cluster health, and moves token ownership carefully when capacity changes.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does the store keep serving traffic during regional failures and cluster resizing?

concepts.definition: A unit such as node, rack, zone, or region that can fail together.
concepts.whyItMatters: Replica placement must avoid correlated failures.

concepts.definition: Moving token ownership and data when nodes enter or leave.
concepts.whyItMatters: It provides scaling without a full redistribution event.

concepts.definition: Replicating data across data centers or regions.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves disaster recovery and can lower local read latency.

deepDives: Cross-region quorum writes are expensive. Most Dynamo-style systems use local quorums plus asynchronous inter-region replication unless the product requires stronger global guarantees.
bottlenecks: Regional replication lag can expose stale reads after failover.
Rebalancing can overload healthy nodes if not rate-limited.
talkingPoints: Close by summarizing the write path and read path end to end.

functional.requirement: Store and retrieve values by key.
functional.how: The API accepts opaque values, coordinators route keys through the token ring, and replicas persist data through commit logs, memtables, and SSTables before returning version context.
Consistent hashing with virtual nodes lets new machines take bounded token ranges, while the rebalance controller moves only affected data.
Replica sets, tunable quorums, sloppy quorum behavior, and hinted handoff keep writes available during temporary node failures.
Vector clocks expose concurrent writes to clients, read repair fixes hot stale replicas, and Merkle-tree anti-entropy repairs cold ranges.
Regional routing, replica placement across failure domains, metrics, and async cross-region replication allow failover with explicit freshness tradeoffs.

nonFunctional.how: Append-only local writes, bounded quorum choices, Bloom filters, and cache-friendly SSTable reads keep the foreground path short.
Virtual nodes and versioned ring metadata allow capacity changes without remapping the whole keyspace.
Gossip, hints, read repair, anti-entropy jobs, and repair metrics make eventual consistency observable and recoverable.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a Dynamo-style distributed key-value store. Clients call any coordinator, coordinators route keys through a virtual-node ring, replica sets execute tunable quorum reads and writes, each replica stores data in a log-structured engine, vector clocks expose concurrent versions, and repair systems converge replicas after failures.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk the interviewer through a put and a get: which node coordinates it, how replicas are chosen, when the client receives success, and how the system later repairs divergence.

concepts.definition: Any healthy node can accept a request and route it to the replica set for the key.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps clients simple and lets the cluster absorb node changes.

concepts.definition: Hints, read repair, and anti-entropy continuously move replicas toward the same state.
concepts.whyItMatters: An available eventually consistent store is only credible if the repair story is explicit.

concepts.definition: R and W determine how many replicas must respond before a read or write completes.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes the CAP tradeoff an API and operations choice rather than a vague slogan.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Stronger consistency variant
options.description: A leader or consensus-backed variant gives stronger per-key semantics but gives up some of the availability and latency benefits of the Dynamo-style default.

flows.name: Quorum write with local durability
flows.note: A successful write is durable on enough replicas; slower replicas and failed replicas are handled after the response.

messages.label: append to commit log and memtable
append to commit log and memtable

flows.name: Read, conflict detection, and repair
flows.note: Reads gather enough versions, return siblings if needed, and opportunistically repair stale replicas.

deepDives: The full design is not one data path. It is request routing, local storage, replication, conflict handling, failure detection, hinted handoff, repair, rebalancing, and cross-region disaster recovery working together.
bottlenecks: Hot keys can overload one replica set.
Anti-entropy and compaction compete with foreground traffic.
Cross-region replication lag affects failover freshness.
talkingPoints: Name N, R, and W for the default.
Explain why vector clocks are needed only because the design accepts writes during partitions.
Close by distinguishing temporary failure recovery from permanent anti-entropy repair.

followUps: How would you support range scans without destroying the hash-based placement model?
What metrics show that anti-entropy repair is falling behind?
How would you protect hot keys?
When would you choose last-write-wins despite the conflict risk?

api.description: Create or replace a value with optional consistency settings.
Read a value and return one or more versions when conflicts exist.
Write a tombstone so deletes replicate and repair consistently.
Inspect partition ownership and replica placement.
